children.name: Plethodon
children.description: The largest genus of salamanders. Breathe entirely through moist skin and the mouth lining — no lungs at all. Eggs laid in terrestrial sites hatch as miniature adults, bypassing the aquatic larval stage. Among the most abundant vertebrates in Appalachian deciduous forest.

children.name: Plethodon glutinosus
children.description: Secretes a sticky mucus that glues the jaws of predators together; among the most abundant vertebrates in Appalachian forest.

children.name: Plethodon cinereus
children.description: One of the most numerous vertebrates in northeastern North American forests; estimated at up to 3 per square metre in optimal habitat; no aquatic stage.

children.name: Eurycea lucifuga
children.description: Vivid orange with black spots; inhabits cave entrances and twilight zones of Appalachian limestone caves.

children.name: Eurycea rathbuni
children.description: Translucent skin, vestigial eyes, bright red external gills; lives entirely in the Edwards Plateau aquifer; critically endangered.

children.name: Hydromantes platycephalus
children.description: Fires a ballistic tongue longer than its own body to catch prey in under 20 milliseconds; bare granite specialist in the Sierra Nevada.

children.name: Hydromantes italicus
children.description: Sole plethodontid in Europe; Tertiary relic isolated in Italian cave systems; IUCN Vulnerable.

children.name: Aneides lugubris
children.description: Climbs trees; prehensile tail; strong jaw muscles deliver a painful bite; California oaks.

children.name: Desmognathus ochrophaeus
children.description: Semi-aquatic; common along Appalachian streams; lays terrestrial egg clutches.
